Q1: I tried to build a consistent routine, honestly I did. I'd block out time in the afternoons after my morning shifts at work, usually around 16 hours spread across the week. Being a kinesthetic learner, I needed to physically write things out and work through problems with my hands, so I'd set up at my small desk with all my materials spread around me. The routine helped manage my anxiety because knowing when I'd study gave me some sense of control.
Q2: I really tried to keep regular times, but life doesn't always cooperate when you're working with limited resources. Deadlines definitely pulled me away from my planned schedule sometimes. When assignments were due, I'd shift everything around and study more intensely in the days before. But I always came back to my routine afterward because the structure helped keep my anxiety from spiraling.
Q3: Usually I logged in to check if there were new assignments posted or to watch the lecture videos. With five online courses, I had to stay on top of things or I'd fall behind quickly. Sometimes the anxiety would hit and I'd log in just to reassure myself I hadn't missed something important. The notifications helped prompt me too, though sometimes they added to my stress.
Q4: Yes, after the first few weeks it became automatic. Every morning I'd check the LMS with my coffee before work, and then again in the afternoon before my study sessions. It became like checking my phone for messages - just something I did without thinking. I think the habit formed because I was genuinely worried about missing deadlines, so the repetition gave me peace of mind.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly valuable because I could pause and rewind when I needed to physically work through concepts. I watched over 190 minutes of videos because I'd often go back to sections multiple times. The assignment submissions were also crucial because they gave me concrete tasks to complete, which suits my hands-on learning style. Seeing my completion rate stay high helped me feel like I was making progress even when the material was difficult.
Q6: I didn't engage much with the discussion forums - I only posted twice the whole course. It's not that I didn't value other perspectives, but typing out my thoughts felt disconnected from how I actually learn. I also tended to skip supplementary readings if they weren't directly tied to assignments. With limited time and resources, I had to prioritize what would directly help my grades.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the assignments or exams. I needed to feel like I was actively doing something, not just passively absorbing information. If I could work through problems or apply concepts practically, I stayed engaged. The moment something felt purely theoretical with no clear application, my attention would drift.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford. Required ones had deadlines and consequences, so they automatically got priority. Optional stuff, even if interesting, would get pushed aside when I was managing my anxiety about the mandatory work. Sometimes I'd feel guilty about skipping them, but I had to be realistic about my time and energy.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real learning happened offline. I'd download the lecture slides and PDFs, then print them out at the library when I could afford it. I'd physically annotate everything, make handwritten flashcards, and work through practice problems on paper. My notebook was covered in diagrams and notes that no system could ever track. That tactile engagement is how information actually sticks for me.
Q10: There were entire evenings where I'd spend three or four hours working through problems by hand, rewriting notes, and testing myself with flashcards I'd made. None of that showed up anywhere in the LMS. I'd also study with a couple of classmates in person sometimes, quizzing each other and explaining concepts out loud. The system only saw that I'd downloaded a file once, not that I'd spent hours with it afterward.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a video or document once, download it, and then that material became part of my physical study space. I'd return to printed notes and downloaded videos repeatedly over weeks. The LMS would show one view, but that resource might have been central to my studying for the entire semester. It's just more practical for me to work with materials offline.
Q12: Around midterms, my anxiety spiked really badly. I was juggling all five courses, worried about money, and the exam content felt overwhelming. There were a few days where I'd log in, see everything I needed to do, and feel paralyzed. The CBT sessions I started helped me work through those moments, teaching me to break things into smaller pieces. But those difficult periods definitely affected my engagement.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about bills, the LMS became harder to face. Sometimes I'd log in with good intentions but my brain just couldn't process the material. Competing responsibilities meant I had to triage constantly - the course with the nearest deadline got attention, others had to wait. Fatigue especially impacted my ability to engage meaningfully with videos or readings.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. Sometimes I'd have a video playing but my mind would be racing with anxiety about other things. I'd realize I'd watched ten minutes without absorbing anything and have to restart. Or I'd scroll through readings without really processing them because I was too tired. The activity was there, but the learning wasn't happening.
Q15: I postponed exam review more than I should have, even though I knew my exam scores were my weakness. The anxiety around exams was intense, so I'd focus on assignments where I felt more confident and capable. It was avoidance, I know that now from my CBT work. The assignments felt manageable and gave me quick wins, while exam prep felt like facing potential failure.
Q16: Deadlines were my primary driver, honestly. I'd structure my entire week around what was due when. My LMS activity probably spiked in the days before submissions. The deadlines gave me external structure that helped contain my anxiety - I knew exactly what I needed to accomplish by when. Without them, I might have spiraled into overwhelm about where to even start.
Q17: Feedback motivated me when it was constructive and specific. Good feedback on assignments made me want to engage more with similar tasks. But waiting for feedback was anxiety-inducing - I'd check the LMS repeatedly hoping to see my grades. When feedback was delayed or vague, it was harder to know if my approach was working, which made me second-guess my study methods.
Q18: The course structure with clear modules and deadlines helped me enormously. I could see the path forward and plan accordingly. Having regular assignment submissions kept me accountable and engaged throughout the semester rather than just cramming at the end. The structure gave my anxious brain something concrete to hold onto instead of spiraling into uncertainty.
Q19: The video content being available anytime was great for my schedule and learning style. But the exam format was harder for me - I'm better at demonstrating knowledge through doing than through timed written tests. The discussion requirements felt forced and didn't match how I naturally engage with material. More hands-on assessment options would have helped me show what I actually learned.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. I was highly active - logging in frequently, watching videos, completing tasks - but my exam performance didn't reflect that effort. For me, it was anxiety affecting test performance and the mismatch between how I learn and how I was assessed. Someone might be genuinely engaging and learning but struggling to demonstrate that knowledge in specific formats.
Q21: That wasn't really my experience since I was pretty active in the LMS. But I can see how someone who learns well independently or has strong external resources might not need the LMS as much. Some people are just efficient - they get what they need quickly and move on. The LMS activity level doesn't necessarily correlate with understanding or capability.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns - when you're active, what you access, completion rates. They capture the visible behaviors pretty well. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, the offline work, the emotional state behind the clicks. They can't see that I watched a video three times because I was anxious, not because I was deeply engaged. They miss the learning that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively wrestling with the material, not just going through motions. It's when I'm making connections, asking questions in my head, wanting to understand rather than just complete. For me, it often looks like physical interaction - writing, drawing diagrams, working through problems. True engagement is when I lose track of time because I'm genuinely absorbed in learning something.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I fought to stay engaged despite my anxiety. It can't capture the hours of offline studying, the handwritten notes, the physical flashcards worn from use. It doesn't know about the CBT sessions that helped me keep going when I wanted to give up. My resilience, my determination to climb despite limited resources - none of that shows in login frequencies or video minutes. The data shows I failed, but it doesn't show how much I actually learned or how hard I tried.
